topic.title: HTML Media and Time Semantics
topic.description: figure and figcaption caption media content; time provides machine-readable dates and durations.

content.text: Media and Time Semantics: figure, figcaption, and time

text.value: HTML provides three dedicated elements for annotating media and temporal data. The
element wraps self-contained content referenced from the main flow: images, diagrams, code listings, charts, and quotations. The
element is the optional caption for its parent figure, placed as its first or last child. The
element marks up a date, time, or duration in human-readable form and provides a machine-readable equivalent via the

by making the semantic association between media and its caption, and between displayed dates and their machine-parseable format, explicit in the HTML rather than implied by visual proximity. Reference:

rows: Wraps self-contained media referenced from the main text
Caption or legend for the parent figure
Phrasing content; inside any flow element

content.title: figure Is Not Only for Images
content.text: The figure element is appropriate for any self-contained content that the main text references but that could be moved without breaking the prose flow: images, SVG diagrams, code listings, data tables, block quotes with attribution, audio players, and video embeds. If the main text says 'as shown in the diagram below' or 'see listing 3', those targets belong in figure elements.

examples.title: figure and figcaption for Images, Code, and Quotes
examples.description: This example shows figure and figcaption used for five content types: a photograph with a caption, an SVG diagram, a code listing, a data table, and a blockquote with attribution. It demonstrates correct placement of figcaption as first or last child, the relationship between figure and main text references, and the CSS needed to style captions accessibly.
examples.code_snippet:
figure and figcaption Patterns

The examples below demonstrate the five content types that belong inside a <figure> element. The main text references each figure, and the caption provides context that supplements the content without duplicating it.

1. Photograph with caption

The HTML5 document structure, illustrated in Figure 1, maps directly to the ARIA landmark roles used by screen readers.

2. Code listing with caption

Listing 1 shows the minimal accessible button pattern using the correct element, type attribute, and label.

3. SVG diagram with caption

The CSS box model, shown in Figure 2, consists of four nested rectangles representing content, padding, border, and margin.

4. Data table with caption

Course enrolment figures for Q1 2025 are summarised in Table 1.

5. Blockquote with attribution
examples.output: Page renders five figure patterns. The image figure shows a placeholder image with a structured caption including a bold figure number. The code listing figure shows a dark code block with the caption above the code in a lighter dark header. The SVG diagram figure shows a CSS box model with four nested coloured rectangles and a caption below. The data table figure shows an enrolment table with a numbered caption above. The blockquote figure shows an indented italic quote with a left blue border and an attribution line in the figcaption.

examples.title: time Element with All datetime Formats
examples.description: This example shows every valid datetime attribute format: date only, date and time, time only, month only, week, year, duration, and timezone offset. It also demonstrates practical use cases: article publish dates, event schedules, relative time labels, and duration indicators.
examples.code_snippet:
time Element: All datetime Formats

1. datetime attribute format reference

Format Example datetime value Human-readable text Use for
Date only 2025-03-01 1 March 2025 Article publish dates, event dates
Date + local time 2025-03-01T09:00 9:00 AM, 1 March Event start times without timezone
Date + time + UTC offset 2025-03-01T09:00+05:30 9:00 AM IST Events with explicit timezone (IST = +05:30)
Date + UTC time 2025-03-01T03:30Z 3:30 UTC Server timestamps, API-sourced datetimes
Time only 09:00 9 AM Recurring daily times (office hours, broadcast)
Year and month 2025-03 March 2025 Monthly reports, billing cycles
Week number 2025-W10 Week 10, 2025 Sprint dates, weekly reports
Year only 2025 2025 Copyright years, annual statistics
Duration PT2H30M 2 hours 30 minutes Course length, video runtime, read time
Duration (days) P3D 3 days Delivery time, trial period, warranty

2. Article cards with publish dates

By Priya Sharma · Published 1 March 2025 · 8 min read

Flexbox vs CSS Grid: When to Use Each

By Arjun Nair · Published 14 February 2025 · 12 min read

Flexbox and Grid solve different layout problems. Choosing the right tool prevents unnecessary complexity.

5. Wrong vs right datetime usage

Wrong: Last updated: yesterday — no datetime, no machine-readable value

Right: Last updated: 28 February 2025 — datetime preserves the date regardless of when it is read
examples.output: Page renders five sections. The format reference table shows all ten datetime formats as live time elements with their machine-readable value and human display text side by side. The article cards section shows two blog post cards with publish dates and read-time durations using time elements. The event schedule shows three time-tagged events with duration badges. The copyright section shows a simple year in a time element. The wrong versus right section shows the relative 'yesterday' anti-pattern versus a fixed ISO 8601 date.

subtopics.title: Figure and Figcaption Elements
subtopics.description: figure wraps referenced media and figcaption provides its visible caption as first or last child.

text.value: element represents a unit of self-contained content, typically referenced from the main text, that could be relocated without disrupting the document flow. It may contain images, SVG, video, audio, code listings, tables, or block quotes. The optional
element provides a visible caption or legend for the figure and must appear as the
is valid; the caption is optional. The pairing creates a structural relationship between the content and its description that screen readers and search engines can interpret, satisfying

content.title: figcaption and alt Text Must Not Duplicate
content.text: The alt attribute on img is a text replacement shown when the image cannot load or is read by a screen reader in place of the image. figcaption is visible supplementary text shown to all users alongside the image. When both are present, a screen reader announces: image description (alt), then caption (figcaption). Writing the same sentence in both causes the same information to be announced twice. alt describes what the image shows; figcaption explains why it is there or provides attribution.

examples.title: Figure with Image, Code Listing, and Blockquote
examples.description: This example shows the figure element used for an image with a supplement caption, a numbered code listing with caption above, and a blockquote with source attribution in figcaption. It demonstrates figcaption placement (first vs last child), the non-duplication rule between alt and figcaption, and responsive figure styling.
examples.code_snippet:
figure and figcaption Reference

1. Image with caption (figcaption as last child)

The document landmark structure shown in Figure 1 maps each HTML5 element to its ARIA role.

2. Floated figure in running text

Priya Sharma has taught HTML and CSS to over 12,000 students across India. Her course on semantic HTML has been recognised by the W3C Web Accessibility Initiative as an exemplary learning resource for developers entering the accessibility field.

Her teaching approach emphasises building correct HTML structure before adding CSS or JavaScript, ensuring every project starts with a solid accessible foundation.

3. Code listing with caption above (figcaption as first child)

Listing 1 shows the complete structure of an accessible HTML form field with label, input, and error message.

4. Blockquote with attribution in figcaption

5. figcaption placement: wrong vs right

<!-- WRONG: figcaption not first or last -->
<figure>
  <img src="..." alt="...">
  <figcaption>Caption</figcaption>  <!-- OK: last child -->
  <p>Extra note</p>  <!-- figcaption is no longer last -->
</figure>
<!-- RIGHT: figcaption is the last child -->
<figure>
  <img src="..." alt="...">
  <p>Extra note</p>
  <figcaption>Caption</figcaption>  <!-- last child: valid -->
</figure>
examples.output: Page renders five sections. The image figure shows a landmark diagram with a bold-numbered figcaption below. The floated figure shows an avatar image floating right with the instructor bio text wrapping around it. The code listing figure shows a dark code block with the listing label as a dark header above the code. The blockquote figure shows an indented quote with a left blue border and attribution in figcaption including a cite element. The placement section shows red and green code blocks for wrong and right figcaption positioning.

text.value: is this content part of the text flow, or is it referenced by the text flow?
An image that illustrates a sentence is part of the flow. An image that a sentence points to with 'as shown in Figure 3' is referenced. Referenced content belongs in
; inline content belongs directly in the paragraph.
is parent-child, not proximity-based. A screen reader does not infer that a
is its caption. But it does understand that
is the caption for the figure's content. This is the same reason
is better than visual proximity for form inputs.
) and link to them with fragment anchors (

). This creates a navigable cross-reference system in long documents: clicking 'Figure 2' in the text scrolls to the actual figure, and screen reader users can jump there directly without reading the entire document.

subtopics.title: Time Element
subtopics.description: The time element pairs human-readable date or duration text with a machine-readable datetime value.

format while the text content can be any human-readable representation. The

examples.description: This example shows the time element for article publish dates with ISO 8601 dates, read-time durations with PT notation, a multi-timezone event schedule using UTC offsets, relative display text with fixed datetime values, and the JavaScript pattern for converting datetime attributes to locale-formatted strings.

examples.output: Page renders six sections. The article meta section shows two post cards with publish dates and read-time durations as time elements. The event schedule shows a table with four session rows, each with a full IST datetime value in the time element and a blue duration badge. The relative display section shows a time element with a human-readable date. The JavaScript section shows the Intl.DateTimeFormat pattern reading the datetime attribute and replacing the display text with a locale-formatted string. The year-only section shows copyright and month-year usage. The wrong versus right section shows the ambiguous 'yesterday' without datetime versus the correct ISO date.

seo_metadata.meta_title: HTML Media and Time Semantics | figure, figcaption, time Elements
seo_metadata.meta_description: Learn HTML figure, figcaption, and time elements: captioning images and code listings, alt vs figcaption differences, and ISO 8601 datetime attribute formats.
